---
title: "GMS: Building the Base - Tech Stack and Folder Structure"
subtitle: "Grading Management System"
author: "Yuvraj Rathod"
date: "2026-07-10"
description: "An overview of the technology stack and monorepo folder structure chosen for GMS, detailing the blueprint of its microservices and separation of concerns."
categories: [Software Engineering, Open Source]
format:
  html:
    code-tools: false
    link-external-newwindow: true
---

In the [last blog](../gms_2/index.qmd), I shared how **GMS** was designed as a system. I explained the choice of microservices and how each service would own its own data.

That design gave **GMS** its shape. But now came the harder part: turning that design into a real codebase.

The question was not just what tools to use, but how to organize the code in a way that made sense. Especially because **GMS** was [open source on GitHub](https://github.com/yuvraj-rathod-1202/GMS), the structure had to be clear enough that anyone reading it could understand it quickly.

In this blog, I will share the tech stack I chose and the folder structure I designed. More importantly, I will explain the thinking behind each choice and why this structure worked so well as the project grew.

## The Tech Stack I Used

Before deciding on the folder structure, I had to choose the right tools.

The stack had to match the kind of system I was building. I needed strong backend services, a modern frontend, a solid database, and a clean path to deploy everything. It also had to be a stack that other developers could understand quickly, since the project was open source.

The main stack became:

- **Python and FastAPI** for backend services
- **Next.js and TypeScript** for the frontend
- **MySQL** for the database
- **RabbitMQ** for async messaging
- **Docker** for containers
- **Kubernetes** for orchestration

This combination gave me a good balance between speed of development and system structure.

### Why I chose FastAPI and Python

**FastAPI** was a strong choice for several reasons.

First, it is fast to write with. **FastAPI** handles a lot of the boilerplate that other frameworks require. That meant I could focus on business logic instead of framework setup.

Second, **FastAPI** is easy to read. The code stays clean and simple, which matters when you have multiple services and multiple developers working on them.

Third, **FastAPI** works very well for API work. It gives you request validation, automatic documentation, and error handling without extra work. That is important when you have a gateway routing requests to multiple services.

**Python** helped me move quickly while keeping the code simple enough to maintain. Since **GMS** is [open source on GitHub](https://github.com/yuvraj-rathod-1202/GMS), readability is just as important as speed.

### Why I chose Next.js and TypeScript for the frontend

The frontend needed to be modern, flexible, and easy to grow. Next.js gave me all of that.

It allowed me to build with a component-based approach, which made it much easier to keep the UI organized. It also gave me built-in routing, static generation, and server-side rendering, which helped with performance.

Using **TypeScript** added another layer of safety. It caught errors early, made refactoring easier, and the code was much clearer to read.

### Why MySQL

**MySQL** was chosen because it is reliable, widely understood, and works well with the microservices approach. Each service could own its own database schema without having to coordinate with other services.

**MySQL** is also mature and well-documented, which matters when building an [open source project on GitHub](https://github.com/yuvraj-rathod-1202/GMS). Teams can find help easily, and new contributors do not have to learn a new database system.

## Thinking About Folder Structure for Production

Once I had the tech stack, I had to think about folder structure.

This might sound like a small choice, but it matters a lot when a project grows. When **GMS** goes into production and gets used at real courses, the folder structure has to make sense. Every developer who works on it has to understand where code belongs.

It matters even more because **GMS** is [open source on GitHub](https://github.com/yuvraj-rathod-1202/GMS). If someone new reads the code, they should be able to navigate it without confusion. A good folder structure is like a map. It helps people find what they need.

So I spent time thinking about this. The question was: how do I organize the code so that it makes sense both to me and to future contributors?

The answer came from the system design I had already done. I had already decided that **GMS** would be built as microservices. That meant I should organize the code around those services.

## The Monorepo Approach: One Folder Per Service

I decided to keep **GMS** as a monorepo, with one folder per service. This meant I would not split the code into separate Git repositories. Instead, everything lived in one place, with each service in its own folder.

This approach had clear benefits:

- It was easy to see all the services in one place
- Deployment scripts could work with the whole project
- Contributors could understand the full system without cloning multiple repos
- Changes across services were easier to manage

The main folders became:

- `gateway` - The entry point for all requests
- `auth_user` - Login and user identity
- `courses` - Course management
- `marks` - Assessments and mark entry
- `policy` - Grading rules and policies
- `analytics` - Course and assessment analytics
- `frontend` - The user interface
- `mysql` - Database setup and scripts
- `rabbitmq` - Messaging configuration
- `docs` - Documentation
- `scripts` - Shared deployment scripts

This structure made ownership clear. If I needed to work on `auth`, I knew to go to the `auth_user` folder. If I needed course logic, the `courses` folder was where to look.

## The Blueprint of a Microservice

Now came the real design work. Each backend service had to have a clear internal structure.

I wanted all services to follow the same pattern. This meant that if a developer understood one service, they could understand all the others. But I also wanted each service to have the freedom to grow in its own way.

So I designed a blueprint that every backend service would follow:

```
service_name/
├── Dockerfile
├── server.py
├── requirements.txt
├── init.sql
├── manifests/
│   ├── deploy.yaml
│   ├── service.yaml
│   └── config.yaml
├── models/
│   └── schema/
│       ├── request.py
│       └── response.py
├── routes/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── [endpoint_name].py
├── services/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── [logic_name].py
├── utils/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── db.py
│   ├── auth.py
│   └── [helper_name].py
├── tests/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── conftest.py
│   ├── api/
│   └── services/
└── pytest.ini
```

Here is what each folder and file does:

- **Dockerfile** - Instructions to build and run this service as a container
- **server.py** - The entry point that starts the **FastAPI** application
- **requirements.txt** - Lists all **Python** packages needed by this service
- **init.sql** - Database initialization script that creates tables and schema for this service
- **manifests/** - **Kubernetes** configuration files (deploy.yaml, service.yaml, config.yaml)
- **models/** - Request and response data schemas using **Pydantic** for validation
- **routes/** - API endpoints that handle HTTP requests (each file is a group of related endpoints)
- **services/** - Business logic layer where the actual work happens
- **utils/** - Helper functions like database connections (db.py), authentication (auth.py), and other utilities
- **tests/** - Tests for the API (api/) and business logic (services/)

## Why This Structure Worked So Well

This blueprint created a clear separation of concerns. Each part of the code had one job:

- **Routes are separate from services** - If I needed to change an API endpoint, I only touched `routes`. The business logic in `services` stayed the same. Changes were isolated and easy to track.

- **Services are separate from utilities** - If I needed to change how the database works, I only changed `db.py` in utils. Every service using the database automatically got the improvement.

- **Each service is independent** - With its own Dockerfile, requirements.txt, and database schema, each service could be developed, tested, and deployed separately without affecting others.

The biggest win was **loose coupling**. Services worked together but did not depend heavily on each other. If one part changed, the others did not break.

This meant:

- New developers only had to understand one service to start working
- Teams could work on different services at the same time
- I could replace parts of the system without breaking everything else
- The codebase stayed flexible and easy to improve over time

## Closing Thoughts

The base of **GMS** was not just the tech stack. It was the careful structure I built around the stack.

The choice of **Python**, **FastAPI**, **Next.js**, and **MySQL** gave me the right tools. But the folder structure I designed gave the code its shape.

By organizing code around services and by keeping each part focused on one job, I created a system that was:

- Easy to understand
- Easy to maintain
- Easy to grow
- Easy to contribute to
- Easy to deploy

These qualities mattered because **GMS** was not just a personal project. It was meant to be [open source on GitHub](https://github.com/yuvraj-rathod-1202/GMS). It was meant to be worked on by many different people.

The structure had to support all of that. And it did.

In the next blog, I will share how I turned this blueprint into real code, starting with the first services and how the core system started to take shape.
